title: Branches: Working Without Stepping on Each Other

content.explanation: A branch is an independent line of work in a repo. Instead of everyone editing the same shared history directly, each person (or each feature) works on its own branch, and changes only join main once they're reviewed and merged — so one person's half-finished work never blocks or breaks anyone else's.
content.example:
git checkout -b feature-ntp-check
# now on branch feature-ntp-check, isolated from main
... make changes, commit ...
git push origin feature-ntp-check
# open a pull request to merge it back into main once it's reviewed

check.statement: What single command creates a new branch called feature-ntp-check AND switches to it?

solution.explanation: 'git checkout -b <name>' is the create-and-switch shortcut — 'git branch <name>' only creates the branch without switching to it, so you'd still be committing to your old branch by mistake.
